subject: Statistics
title: The Monty Hall Problem
description: A quick, practical guide to The Monty Hall Problem.

hook.text: You pick door 1. The host opens door 3, revealing a goat. Should you switch to door 2? Yes — always.

buildup.text: You initially had a 1/3 chance of being right. The other two doors held 2/3 of the probability.

discovery.text: The host always reveals a goat. The 2/3 probability collapses onto the remaining door. Switching wins 2/3 of the time.

twist.text: Nearly 1,000 PhD holders wrote to Marilyn vos Savant saying she was wrong. She was right. They were fooled.

climax.text: The key insight: the host's action gives you information. Not switching ignores free information.

punchline.text: Switch doors. The host just told you where the car probably is.

quiz.question: Why does switching doors in the Monty Hall problem improve your odds?
options: The host's reveal concentrates the 2/3 probability onto the remaining unchosen door
The car is always behind the last door
